## [03:01 - 06:03]

where he gains the power of self-esteem and he sort of grabs it out of the air. And I feel like that's what I wanna do for people is to be like, you have just asked a question that is only asked by people who really are thinking about storytelling in a deep and in fundamental way. So what Susan asks is a great question, which is what do you do at the end of the story so that you're still in the story so that the movie continues to play in the minds of the audience while you're still landing the thing, the idea, the five second moment that you were trying to land? Well, there's a couple of things to think about. One is sometimes people say, I think it's fine to step out of the story and do that. And I think if you're delivering a talk that contains a story, that might be okay. But if you're just telling a story, like I told a story last night at the Moth, that's not okay because I'm not here to sermonize or provide a lesson or a moral or those kinds of things. So I think we run into a hazard when we step out of the story that our story now becomes a talk and our talk can sometimes become a little, what's the word? We'll say sermonizing for lack of a better word. I also remind people that movies never sort of step out of the movie in order to do this, right? At the end of, we'll just pick any movie in the world. At the end of Titanic, when Rose has eschewed all of the trappings of wealth and society and chosen to, hug a blanket on the carpet and leave everyone behind and let them assume she's dead so she can go on and live a life absent of the expectations of social norms. She doesn't turn to the audience and say to the audience, and so now that you've seen the film, you can see that I have really transformed in a meaningful way, right? That would be a weird way to end a movie. And that's sort of, I think, the way some people end stories which is what you've sort of described Susan. So I'm circling the answer, but here's the answer. So last night I told a story about my son Charlie. I was in a baseball stadium when he said something profound and it changed me in a truly fundamental way in a really deep and meaningful way. And when I got to the moment of meaning, sort of the thing I really was waiting to say for the whole story, the purpose of the story, the thing I always do is I land myself back in a scene before I actually say it. So essentially at the very end of the story, my final, let's say 10 sentences, one of those final 10 sentences began with, and so I'm sitting here in this minor league baseball park in this uncomfortable plastic chair next to my son, except now here's what I think and here's what I feel and here's what I see and here's what I say. So I just make sure that the audience can still physically see me in the story and I reestablish location, which is often a location I was already in before and I just wanna remind them that I am still in that location or maybe it's a new location, but I reestablished scene and location

and sort of everything that that encompasses before I say the thing that I need to say. Does that make sense? Yeah, it does, yes. When I'm storytelling as a speaker, I am always very still because I want people to sort of get lost in my story and not be thinking about me in a physical way, but as soon as sort of the story is done and I now need to talk to them and address them, I'll move closer to them in a TED talk, I'll step outside that orange circle if I can and sort of move around a bit.

## [15:07 - 18:09]

your house on your lawn that's not incredibly comfortable and is essentially just wood somehow costs $900. And they have to get chained down in all these places because they cost so much. And so I proposed a whole bunch of jokes that she liked. But the most important thing about the jokes that I was proposing was I told her, you can't just take any jokes because in addition to being funny at the beginning of your story, it's also an opportunity to give your audience a peek into who you are as a person. The type of joke that you make indicates who you are. And so jokes that I make because of who I am are different than the jokes that other people might make. So I see an Adirondack chair and I see an overpriced piece of ridiculous arcana that sits on people's lawns and costs more money than insert funny thing that we can compare it to, right? That's a true statement and that probably would be funny. But what I have learned in working with business folks who want me to help them with humor is sometimes my friend, Moshe, will say, can we make this funny and can we make it not like a sarcastic, ironic white guy funny? Because that's the humor that I often offer, which is my brand of humor, which is to say the way I see the world. So when I offered Jenny humorous ideas about the Adirondack chairs, I was careful to tell her, make sure you choose to be funny in the way that you see the world. Don't just take my jokes. Take my sort of, my invitation to explore the landscape of Adirondack chairs in the way that both is funny, but also illustrates who you are as a person. I think too often storytellers and actually comedians, they will latch onto something that is funny, but is not actually indicative of who they are as a person. And I think that's not a helpful version of humor. I hear a lot of standup comics these days love to talk about struggles with mental illness, their incapacity to deal with the world, their struggles with stress and anxiety. It's a very popular topic with standup comedians. And I must assume that it is popular with them because they are struggling with these things. As a person who does standup comedy myself, it would also be easy for me to make jokes about my incapacity and my anxiety and my stress with the world and my fears and my overwhelming sense of dread, but I don't actually have any of those things. So I know I can make jokes about those things, but they are not indicative of the human being that I am. In the same way, I can't make jokes about the dating scene because I haven't been dating for 20 years. I've never used an app to date. ## [18:09 - 21:13]

So the ultimate version of humor, whether you're doing standup or telling a story or infusing a talk with humor, the ultimate version of humor is the thing that makes people funny while simultaneously gives people a peak into sort of who you are, how you view the world, what your disposition is, what your nature is, who you are as a human being. And so Jenny understood that because she's a genius and she took that challenge on. And she found some Adirondack chair jokes that were more indicative of how she felt than I felt, which honestly, we're about the same because we tend to think the same in a lot of ways. My jokes tend to work well for her too. And vice versa, when she gives me something funny, it's often also reflective of how I feel. But that's an important lesson that people have to learn. It's hard to find funny things. And so people will just find a funny thing that really doesn't relate to them and they will decide, I'm gonna use that joke. And I say, but that's not who you are. And they say, I know, but it's funny. I don't wanna make someone laugh. Well, fight for the humor that is best suited for you. Hopefully that makes some sense. So that was one lesson we learned last night. The other thing I guess I'll say, I'll give you one more little bit of advice. Almost every single story last night, with I think the exception of three, began with the phrase, I'm going to tell you a story about. And that is a terrible way to begin a story. And yet I hear it all the time. Some of them said, I'm gonna tell you a story about my mother and how she was my Achilles heel. Now, Achilles heel was the theme. So people liked to use that phrase early in their story and say, say, this is a story about my mother and how she has been my Achilles heel. It's a bad way to begin a story for a bunch of reasons. One is it doesn't activate imagination. Number two, it's a meaningless set of words because we know you're about to tell a story. You're at a story slam. Even if you're sitting at a dinner party with someone and you're about to tell a story, you don't have to say, hey, I'm about to tell a story to your friends. You just start telling the story. The same thing applies when you're on a stage. Don't say I'm about to tell a story or I'm going to tell a story or that this is a story about. Just tell the story, don't waste words. But also if you tell me that this is a story about my mother who was my Achilles heel, you've sort of told me most of the story at that point. You've sort of already given it away because now I know what to look for. I know your mom's gonna come in and I know she's gonna be a pain in the ass in some way. I don't know the particulars and that doesn't mean the story is not gonna be entertaining. But boy, you've stolen a lot of the joy of suspense and surprise and wonder from the story by opening with this is a story about. Another person opened their story by saying, I think this is a story about such and such, but I'm not really sure. So by the end of the story, you're gonna tell me if you think it's a story about this or if it's a story about that. That's also a terrible way to begin a story. Don't give an audience who has arrived to a story slam to be entertained a job at the beginning of your story. I don't wanna have to be told what to listen for and be assigned a task to complete by the end of your story.

It's not a great way to tell a story. It's really a frustrating way to tell a story. And quite frankly, I'm not gonna remember hopefully what you asked me to do at the top of the story if your story is really engrossing. Once your story has really grabbed me, all that nonsense you said in the beginning sort of falls out of my head. It just amounts to wasted time and wasted words. It's the same thing as when someone opens a story with a quote, someone might say like Nietzsche said and they'll give the quote and then they'll say, then they'll begin their story as if the quote is like an epilogue and a book. Or my books have epilogues quite often my novels which is an epilogue that begins the book. But I don't expect by page 367, they're gonna remember what the epilogue is. The epilogue is really just for me more than anybody else. It's like, oh, here's a quote that really relates well to this book. And if you finish my story and you happen to go back and reread it again or you happen to go back and look at the epilogue, now you'll see why it was included there, but we can't do that in storytelling. We can't provide an epilogue or a prologue, an epilogue, an epigraph, an epigraph is the word I'm looking for, not an epilogue, I apologize, epigraph. It's true. Right. My son either through osmosis, genetics, choice, whatever it is, we tend to look at the world in the same way, which is we look at the world and find reasons to be appalled.
